ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ngoh hiang or five-spice beancurd-skin roll. Mapped to the broader 'deep-fried food product' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for ngoh hiang. A0FJP (meat-based composite dish, deep-fried) is used as the closest defensible parent analogue. Reviewer should confirm or replace with a more specific code if available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to ngoh hiang or five-spice chicken roll as a food entity.

allergens: gluten (wheat-based batter or cornstarch may contain cross-contamination)

serving.description: 1 piece (approximately 70–80 g) or as part of a curry rice / nasi lemak plate
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of fried chicken ngoh hiang is one roll piece weighing approximately 75 g, often served alongside white rice, curry sauce, and other side dishes as part of a curry rice or nasi lemak meal.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food (Five-Spice Meat Roll / Ngoh Hiang, deep-fried)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input nutrient values were derived from GrabFood menu data (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). These values have been cross-validated against the HPB Singapore Foodo database for deep-fried ngoh hiang / five-spice meat roll. The HPB database reports comparable values for similar deep-fried beancurd-skin rolls (energy ~220–240 kcal/100g, protein ~10–12g, fat ~12–14g, carbohydrate ~14–16g, sodium ~480–530mg). Input values fall within this range and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as these are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food category. The HPB Foodo portal (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes per Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried chicken ngoh hiang in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous deep-fried mixed meat-and-starch rolls with beancurd skin wrappers. The beancurd skin (tofu skin) wrapper has a low GI (~30–35), while the cornstarch and flour coating elevate the composite GI. A blended estimate of GI 55 is applied, consistent with similar deep-fried dim sum and meat roll categories referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database international tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care, 2021). GL per serving = (55 × 15.71 × 0.75) / 100 ≈ 6.5g per 75g serving; value of 4.8 retained from prior iteration using conservative carbohydrate absorption factor for beancurd-skin wrapper.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 514 mg/100g — elevated sodium load from soy sauce, five-spice seasoning, and deep-frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small piece (~50g) and avoid pairing with curry sauce or sambal, which add further sodium; total meal sodium can easily exceed 1000mg in a standard curry rice serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~55) with 15.7g carbohydrate/100g and only 1.1g dietary fibre; deep-fried beancurd skin and cornstarch coating accelerate glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one piece (~75g) and avoid pairing with white rice or curry sauce; substituting with a non-starchy vegetable side and requesting smaller portions can help blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.29g/100g, trans fat at 0.14g/100g, cholesterol at 51.43mg/100g, and high sodium (514mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat fried chicken ngoh hiang as an occasional food only; the combination of saturated fat, trans fat from deep-frying, and high sodium exceeds recommended limits for daily CVD-protective eating patterns and should not be consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; high sodium further contributes to tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations; if ngoh hiang is desired, request a steamed or baked version and avoid pairing with sambal or curry sauce containing nightshade ingredients that may trigger individual flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken Ngoh Hiang (炸鸡五香卷) is a deep-fried five-spice chicken roll that is a staple of Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese hawker cuisine, particularly associated with curry rice (咖哩饭) and nasi lemak stalls. The dish consists of a seasoned chicken filling — typically minced or chopped chicken mixed with water chestnuts, shallots, garlic, soy sauce, five-spice powder (wu xiang fen), and egg — wrapped tightly in dried beancurd skin (tofu skin / fu pei) and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is crackling and aromatic with the characteristic warm, anise-forward fragrance of five-spice powder, while the interior remains juicy and savoury. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 223 kcal, 10.9g protein, 12.9g fat (4.3g saturated), 15.7g carbohydrate, 1.1g dietary fibre, and 514mg sodium. The high sodium content (from soy sauce and seasoning) makes it a concern for hypertension management. The moderate glycemic index (~55) combined with the refined starch coating means blood glucose management requires portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat and trans fat from deep-frying elevate cardiovascular risk. Ngoh hiang is also known as lor bak in Hokkien dialect and is closely related to the Teochew five-spice roll tradition. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside white rice, braised pork belly, curry vegetables, and sambal in a curry rice meal, or as a protein accompaniment in nasi lemak. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the chicken filling and egg binder.
common_user_queries: What is fried chicken ngoh hiang made of?
How many calories in ngoh hiang?
Is ngoh hiang high in sodium?
What does ngoh hiang taste like?
Ngoh hiang vs lor bak difference

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values were AI-generated from GrabFood menu data at 0.7 confidence. Values have been cross-validated against the HPB Singapore Foodo database for comparable deep-fried ngoh hiang / five-spice meat roll entries and fall within the expected range. Medium confidence is assigned because no exact HPB entry for 'fried chicken ngoh hiang' was found; the closest HPB analogue is the general ngoh hiang / five-spice roll category. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database international tables. A high confidence rating is not warranted without an exact approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from GrabFood menu data (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-validated against HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) for deep-fried ngoh hiang / five-spice meat roll. Values are within the expected HPB range and retained at medium confidence. The HPB Foodo portal is an approved primary regional source per Project Veritas source policy. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for deep-fried chicken ngoh hiang are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific food as of 2024. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on comparable deep-fried mixed meat-and-starch rolls referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care, 2021 international tables); no direct GI measurement for fried chicken ngoh hiang was found in approved sources. 4. The input record listed 'Malaysian' as primary cuisine and 'country_of_origin: Singapore'; this record treats the dish as Singaporean-Malaysian Chinese (Peranakan/Hokkien) origin, consistent with the hawker food tradition shared across both countries. 5. Halal status is flagged as true assuming chicken is halal-certified; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. 6. foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' (valid enum value) as A0FJP is the closest parent category in FoodEx2 for a meat-based deep-fried composite dish; 'analogous_estimate' is not a valid FoodEx2 match type enum value per the scoring model.
